title: Phaeochromocytoma
oneLiner: A rare catecholamine-secreting adrenal tumour causing paroxysmal hypertension, headache, palpitations, sweating; life-threatening if missed due to hypertensive crises or arrhythmias.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hypertensive crisis (BP >180/120) with end-organ damage** — look for headache, visual disturbance, seizures, or chest pain. Single finding: **papilloedema or acute LV failure** (pulmonary oedema on exam).
- **Catecholamine-induced cardiomyopathy / acute myocardial infarction** — profound chest pain + widespread T-wave inversion or ST depression on ECG. Single finding: **troponin rise with normal coronary arteries**.
- **Stroke / intracerebral haemorrhage** — sudden focal neurology with extreme hypertension. Single finding: **CT head shows bleed without other cause**.
- **Arrhythmia (VT, VF, or AF with rapid ventricular response)** — collapse, pulselessness, or palpitations with lightheadedness. Single finding: **ECG shows broad-complex tachycardia or atrial arrhythmia in setting of high BP**.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms**: Paroxysmal episodes of severe headache (often pounding), palpitations (forceful or irregular heartbeat), profuse sweating, pallor, flushing. Episodes last minutes to hours, triggered by exercise, stress, micturition, palpation, certain drugs (e.g., metoclopramide, beta-blockers).
- **Sustained hypertension** ~50% of cases; paroxysmal hypertension in ~30%.
- **Other**: Anxiety, tremor, weight loss, hyperglycaemia, postural hypotension (due to volume contraction).
- **Key exam findings**: Hypertensive retinopathy, tachycardia, pallor, tremor, postural BP drop >20/10 mmHg.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Panic disorder / anxiety** — no hypertension during attacks; normal plasma metanephrines.
- **Essential hypertension** — sustained, not paroxysmal; no catecholamine excess. **Splitter:** Paroxysmal symptoms + raised 24h urinary metanephrines distinguish phaeochromocytoma.
- **Thyrotoxicosis** — weight loss, tremor, tachycardia, sweating but usually no paroxysmal hypertension; suppressed TSH, raised T4.
- **Migraine** — headache can be severe, but no hypertension or catecholamine surges; associated aura.
- **Drug withdrawal (clonidine, beta-blockers)** — hypertension + tachycardia; history of medication use.
- **Renal artery stenosis** — sustained hypertension, abdominal bruit, not paroxysmal; imaging shows stenosis.
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage** — thunderclap headache, hypertension; CT head shows bleed.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside**:
  - ECG (look for LVH, ischaemic changes, arrhythmias)
  - Continuous BP monitoring (note paroxysmal rises)
  - Urine dipstick (proteinuria, glucose)
  - Ambulatory BP monitoring if helpful

- **Bloods**:
  - Plasma free metanephrines (normetanephrine and metanephrine) — first-line biochemical test; >3x upper limit of normal is diagnostic.
  - Serum catecholamines (less sensitive, but can be used)
  - FBC, U&Es, LFTs, glucose (hyperglycaemia common)
  - Calcium (rule out hyperparathyroidism if MEN2 suspicion)
  - Genetic testing if young age, family history, bilateral/multiple tumours (MEN2, VHL, NF1, SDHx)

- **Imaging**:
  - **CT abdomen with contrast** — first-line for adrenal mass; typical: >1 cm, hypervascular, necrotic centre.
  - **MRI adrenal** — if CT contraindicated or to assess extra-adrenal paraganglioma.
  - **123I-MIBG scintigraphy** — functional imaging for staging, detecting metastasis, or confirming residual disease post-op.
  - **PET-CT (68Ga-DOTATATE)** — if MIBG negative or suspicion of metastatic spread.

- **Special tests**:
  - **Clonidine suppression test** — if plasma metanephrines equivocal. Administer clonidine 0.3 mg, measure catecholamines at 0, 1, 2, 3 hours; in phaeochromocytoma, levels do not suppress. (Rarely done; specialist team.)
  - **Genetic counselling and testing** for SDHx, RET, VHL, NF1 when indicated.

- **Diagnostic criteria**:
  - Biochemical evidence: plasma metanephrines >2–3x upper limit of normal, especially normetanephrine.
  - Imaging confirms adrenal or extra-adrenal tumour.
  - Post-surgical histology confirms medullary origin.

- **Never-miss test**: **24-hour urinary metanephrines** (or plasma metanephrines) before any invasive procedure or surgery — missing this can lead to hypertensive crisis during anaesthesia or biopsy.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED)**:
  - If hypertensive crisis: **IV phentolamine** (bolus 1–5 mg) for rapid alpha-blockade. Alternatively **IV labetalol** (caution: beta-blocker before full alpha-block can precipitate crisis). **Avoid uncontested beta-blockade** (e.g., propranolol monotherapy).
  - Control BP with **short-acting alpha-blocker** (phentolamine) or **oral phenoxybenzamine 10 mg** (if able to give oral).
  - Monitor ECG, cardiac enzymes, glucose.
  - Admit to HDU/ICU if evidence of end-organ damage.

- **Ward (first 24–72h)**:
  - **Start alpha-blockade**: phenoxybenzamine 10 mg PO BD, titrate by 10 mg every 2–3 days to achieve normotension (max 1 mg/kg/day). Or **doxazosin** 1–2 mg PO OD, titrate to 4–12 mg daily.
  - **IV fluids** to treat volume contraction (alpha-blockade precipitates hypotension). Crystalloid 1–2 L/24h, guided by BP and urine output.
  - **Beta-blockade** only once alpha-blocked and BP controlled: propranolol 40 mg PO TDS or atenolol 25–50 mg PO OD. Start doses low and titrate.
  - Monitor for orthostatic hypotension, arrhythmias, and glucose.
  - **Pre-operative preparation** continues for 7–14 days: aim for BP <140/90, heart rate 60–80 bpm, no postural drop >20/10.
  - **Surgical referral**: laparoscopic adrenalectomy or open for large/malignant tumours.

- **Long-term / outpatient**:
  - Definitive management: **surgical resection** of tumour.
  - Pre-op: continue alpha-blocker and beta-blocker as above. Consider **metyrosine** (inhibit catecholamine synthesis) if refractory hypertension (specialist only).
  - Post-op: monitor for hypotension (sudden drop catecholamines). Often require IV fluids and vasopressors briefly.
  - **Malignant disease**: MIBG therapy, chemotherapy (cyclophosphamide, vincristine, dacarbazine), or tyrosine kinase inhibitors (sunitinib). Refer to oncology.

- **Default Orders (if admitted)**:
  - **Fluids**: IV 0.9% saline at 1–2 L/24h (maintenance plus replacement for volume contraction).
  - **VTE prophylaxis**: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (unless contraindicated).
  - **Diet/NBM**: Normal diet unless pre-op (then NBM 6h before surgery).
  - **Observations**: NEWS2 every 4h initially, more frequent if unstable. BP monitoring every 1–2h when titrating alpha-blocker. Daily weight and urine output.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents**:
  - **Phenoxybenzamine** (non-selective alpha-blocker): initial 10 mg PO BD, increase by 10 mg every 2–3 days. Max 1 mg/kg/day. Duration: up to 14 days pre-op.
  - **Propranolol** (non-selective beta-blocker): 40 mg PO TDS, titrate to target heart rate 60–80 bpm.
  - Alternative: **Doxazosin** (selective alpha-1 blocker): 1–2 mg PO OD, titrate to 4–12 mg daily.
  - Alternative: **Atenolol** (selective beta-1): 25–50 mg PO OD.

- **Common alternatives**: **Labetalol** (mixed alpha+beta blocker): 100–400 mg PO BD. Can be used alone but risk in acute crisis.

- **Key contraindications / interactions**:
  - Beta-blockers without prior alpha-blockade → hypertensive crisis (unopposed alpha effect).
  - Phenoxybenzamine + vasodilators (e.g., sildenafil) → severe hypotension.
  - Metoclopramide, tricyclic antidepressants, sympathomimetics (e.g., pseudoephedrine) can precipitate crisis.

- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**: No major adjustment for alpha-blockers. Propranolol reduces clearance in hepatic impairment; reduce dose. Atenolol is renally excreted — reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min.

- **Most dangerous dose error**: **Starting beta-blocker (e.g., propranolol) before adequate alpha-blockade** — can cause catastrophic hypertension, stroke, or MI. Always document "alpha-blocked" before adding beta-blocker.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute**:
  - Hypertensive emergency (CVA, MI, aortic dissection, encephalopathy)
  - Arrhythmias (VT, VF, AF)
  - Catecholamine-induced cardiomyopathy
  - Pulmonary oedema
  - Hypotension (post-resection or after alpha-blocker initiation)

- **Chronic**:
  - Persistent hypertension (despite surgery if delayed)
  - Left ventricular hypertrophy
  - Glucose intolerance / diabetes
  - Malignant transformation (10%)
  - Recurrence (especially in syndromic cases)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Benign**: Surgical resection is curative with >95% 5-year survival. Symptoms resolve within weeks. Hypertensive cardiomyopathy may reverse.
- **Malignant**: 5-year survival ~50%. Recurrence common. Requires multidisciplinary oncology input.
- **Mortality** from missed diagnosis: up to 50% if untreated (from cardiovascular events).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always** check 24h urinary metanephrines before any adrenal biopsy or surgery in a patient with hypertension and an adrenal mass.
- **Start alpha-blockade first**, even if normotensive — it prevents intraoperative hypertensive crises. Titrate over 1–2 weeks.
- **Pre-load with IV fluids** before and after starting alpha-blockers — volume contraction is universal.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT** give beta-blocker (e.g., propranolol) without adequate alpha-blockade — this can precipitate a hypertensive emergency.
- **Do NOT** assume a small adrenal adenoma is benign — always rule out phaeochromocytoma with biochemistry before intervention.
- **Do NOT** use metoclopramide for nausea in a patient with undiagnosed phaeochromocytoma — it can trigger a severe crisis.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if**: Severe or "worst-ever" headache, palpitations with chest pain, breathlessness, visual disturbance, seizure, or faint.
- **Return if**: New or worsening hypertension in context of known adrenal mass waiting for surgery.
- **Follow-up**: Endocrinology for pre-op optimisation; surgical team for date of adrenalectomy. Once stable on alpha-blocker, proceed with surgery within 2–4 weeks.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Registrar / SpR) if**:
  - BP >180/110 despite alpha-blocker, especially with headache or visual symptoms.
  - Heart rate >120 bpm with palpitations or chest pain.
  - Any arrhythmia (ECG change) or troponin rise.
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L.
- **Call ICU / Crash team if**:
  - Hypertensive crisis with end-organ failure (e.g., stroke, MI, pulmonary oedema, oliguria).
  - Seizure or GCS <13.
  - Sustained VT or VF.
- **Threshold for ICU admission**: Evidence of catecholamine-induced cardiomyopathy, multi-organ dysfunction, need for continuous IV alpha-blocker or vasopressors post-op.

rawMarkdown:
# Phaeochromocytoma

**One-liner:** A rare catecholamine-secreting adrenal tumour causing paroxysmal hypertension, headache, palpitations, sweating; life-threatening if missed due to hypertensive crises or arrhythmias.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Hypertensive crisis (BP >180/120) with end-organ damage** — look for headache, visual disturbance, seizures, or chest pain. Single finding: **papilloedema or acute LV failure** (pulmonary oedema on exam).
- **Catecholamine-induced cardiomyopathy / acute myocardial infarction** — profound chest pain + widespread T-wave inversion or ST depression on ECG. Single finding: **troponin rise with normal coronary arteries**.
- **Stroke / intracerebral haemorrhage** — sudden focal neurology with extreme hypertension. Single finding: **CT head shows bleed without other cause**.
- **Arrhythmia (VT, VF, or AF with rapid ventricular response)** — collapse, pulselessness, or palpitations with lightheadedness. Single finding: **ECG shows broad-complex tachycardia or atrial arrhythmia in setting of high BP**.

### Presentation

- **Cardinal symptoms**: Paroxysmal episodes of severe headache (often pounding), palpitations (forceful or irregular heartbeat), profuse sweating, pallor, flushing. Episodes last minutes to hours, triggered by exercise, stress, micturition, palpation, certain drugs (e.g., metoclopramide, beta-blockers).
- **Sustained hypertension** ~50% of cases; paroxysmal hypertension in ~30%.
- **Other**: Anxiety, tremor, weight loss, hyperglycaemia, postural hypotension (due to volume contraction).
- **Key exam findings**: Hypertensive retinopathy, tachycardia, pallor, tremor, postural BP drop >20/10 mmHg.

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Panic disorder / anxiety** — no hypertension during attacks; normal plasma metanephrines.
- **Essential hypertension** — sustained, not paroxysmal; no catecholamine excess. **Splitter:** Paroxysmal symptoms + raised 24h urinary metanephrines distinguish phaeochromocytoma.
- **Thyrotoxicosis** — weight loss, tremor, tachycardia, sweating but usually no paroxysmal hypertension; suppressed TSH, raised T4.
- **Migraine** — headache can be severe, but no hypertension or catecholamine surges; associated aura.
- **Drug withdrawal (clonidine, beta-blockers)** — hypertension + tachycardia; history of medication use.
- **Renal artery stenosis** — sustained hypertension, abdominal bruit, not paroxysmal; imaging shows stenosis.
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage** — thunderclap headache, hypertension; CT head shows bleed.

### Investigations

- **Bedside**:
  - ECG (look for LVH, ischaemic changes, arrhythmias)
  - Continuous BP monitoring (note paroxysmal rises)
  - Urine dipstick (proteinuria, glucose)
  - Ambulatory BP monitoring if helpful

- **Bloods**:
  - Plasma free metanephrines (normetanephrine and metanephrine) — first-line biochemical test; >3x upper limit of normal is diagnostic.
  - Serum catecholamines (less sensitive, but can be used)
  - FBC, U&Es, LFTs, glucose (hyperglycaemia common)
  - Calcium (rule out hyperparathyroidism if MEN2 suspicion)
  - Genetic testing if young age, family history, bilateral/multiple tumours (MEN2, VHL, NF1, SDHx)

- **Imaging**:
  - **CT abdomen with contrast** — first-line for adrenal mass; typical: >1 cm, hypervascular, necrotic centre.
  - **MRI adrenal** — if CT contraindicated or to assess extra-adrenal paraganglioma.
  - **123I-MIBG scintigraphy** — functional imaging for staging, detecting metastasis, or confirming residual disease post-op.
  - **PET-CT (68Ga-DOTATATE)** — if MIBG negative or suspicion of metastatic spread.

- **Special tests**:
  - **Clonidine suppression test** — if plasma metanephrines equivocal. Administer clonidine 0.3 mg, measure catecholamines at 0, 1, 2, 3 hours; in phaeochromocytoma, levels do not suppress. (Rarely done; specialist team.)
  - **Genetic counselling and testing** for SDHx, RET, VHL, NF1 when indicated.

- **Diagnostic criteria**:
  - Biochemical evidence: plasma metanephrines >2–3x upper limit of normal, especially normetanephrine.
  - Imaging confirms adrenal or extra-adrenal tumour.
  - Post-surgical histology confirms medullary origin.

- **Never-miss test**: **24-hour urinary metanephrines** (or plasma metanephrines) before any invasive procedure or surgery — missing this can lead to hypertensive crisis during anaesthesia or biopsy.

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED)**:
  - If hypertensive crisis: **IV phentolamine** (bolus 1–5 mg) for rapid alpha-blockade. Alternatively **IV labetalol** (caution: beta-blocker before full alpha-block can precipitate crisis). **Avoid uncontested beta-blockade** (e.g., propranolol monotherapy).
  - Control BP with **short-acting alpha-blocker** (phentolamine) or **oral phenoxybenzamine 10 mg** (if able to give oral).
  - Monitor ECG, cardiac enzymes, glucose.
  - Admit to HDU/ICU if evidence of end-organ damage.

- **Ward (first 24–72h)**:
  - **Start alpha-blockade**: phenoxybenzamine 10 mg PO BD, titrate by 10 mg every 2–3 days to achieve normotension (max 1 mg/kg/day). Or **doxazosin** 1–2 mg PO OD, titrate to 4–12 mg daily.
  - **IV fluids** to treat volume contraction (alpha-blockade precipitates hypotension). Crystalloid 1–2 L/24h, guided by BP and urine output.
  - **Beta-blockade** only once alpha-blocked and BP controlled: propranolol 40 mg PO TDS or atenolol 25–50 mg PO OD. Start doses low and titrate.
  - Monitor for orthostatic hypotension, arrhythmias, and glucose.
  - **Pre-operative preparation** continues for 7–14 days: aim for BP <140/90, heart rate 60–80 bpm, no postural drop >20/10.
  - **Surgical referral**: laparoscopic adrenalectomy or open for large/malignant tumours.

- **Long-term / outpatient**:
  - Definitive management: **surgical resection** of tumour.
  - Pre-op: continue alpha-blocker and beta-blocker as above. Consider **metyrosine** (inhibit catecholamine synthesis) if refractory hypertension (specialist only).
  - Post-op: monitor for hypotension (sudden drop catecholamines). Often require IV fluids and vasopressors briefly.
  - **Malignant disease**: MIBG therapy, chemotherapy (cyclophosphamide, vincristine, dacarbazine), or tyrosine kinase inhibitors (sunitinib). Refer to oncology.

- **Default Orders (if admitted)**:
  - **Fluids**: IV 0.9% saline at 1–2 L/24h (maintenance plus replacement for volume contraction).
  - **VTE prophylaxis**: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (unless contraindicated).
  - **Diet/NBM**: Normal diet unless pre-op (then NBM 6h before surgery).
  - **Observations**: NEWS2 every 4h initially, more frequent if unstable. BP monitoring every 1–2h when titrating alpha-blocker. Daily weight and urine output.

### Drugs & Doses

- **First-line agents**:
  - **Phenoxybenzamine** (non-selective alpha-blocker): initial 10 mg PO BD, increase by 10 mg every 2–3 days. Max 1 mg/kg/day. Duration: up to 14 days pre-op.
  - **Propranolol** (non-selective beta-blocker): 40 mg PO TDS, titrate to target heart rate 60–80 bpm.
  - Alternative: **Doxazosin** (selective alpha-1 blocker): 1–2 mg PO OD, titrate to 4–12 mg daily.
  - Alternative: **Atenolol** (selective beta-1): 25–50 mg PO OD.

- **Common alternatives**: **Labetalol** (mixed alpha+beta blocker): 100–400 mg PO BD. Can be used alone but risk in acute crisis.

- **Key contraindications / interactions**:
  - Beta-blockers without prior alpha-blockade → hypertensive crisis (unopposed alpha effect).
  - Phenoxybenzamine + vasodilators (e.g., sildenafil) → severe hypotension.
  - Metoclopramide, tricyclic antidepressants, sympathomimetics (e.g., pseudoephedrine) can precipitate crisis.

- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**: No major adjustment for alpha-blockers. Propranolol reduces clearance in hepatic impairment; reduce dose. Atenolol is renally excreted — reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min.

- **Most dangerous dose error**: **Starting beta-blocker (e.g., propranolol) before adequate alpha-blockade** — can cause catastrophic hypertension, stroke, or MI. Always document "alpha-blocked" before adding beta-blocker.

### Complications

- **Acute**:
  - Hypertensive emergency (CVA, MI, aortic dissection, encephalopathy)
  - Arrhythmias (VT, VF, AF)
  - Catecholamine-induced cardiomyopathy
  - Pulmonary oedema
  - Hypotension (post-resection or after alpha-blocker initiation)

- **Chronic**:
  - Persistent hypertension (despite surgery if delayed)
  - Left ventricular hypertrophy
  - Glucose intolerance / diabetes
  - Malignant transformation (10%)
  - Recurrence (especially in syndromic cases)

### Prognosis

- **Benign**: Surgical resection is curative with >95% 5-year survival. Symptoms resolve within weeks. Hypertensive cardiomyopathy may reverse.
- **Malignant**: 5-year survival ~50%. Recurrence common. Requires multidisciplinary oncology input.
- **Mortality** from missed diagnosis: up to 50% if untreated (from cardiovascular events).

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always** check 24h urinary metanephrines before any adrenal biopsy or surgery in a patient with hypertension and an adrenal mass.
- **Start alpha-blockade first**, even if normotensive — it prevents intraoperative hypertensive crises. Titrate over 1–2 weeks.
- **Pre-load with IV fluids** before and after starting alpha-blockers — volume contraction is universal.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Do NOT** give beta-blocker (e.g., propranolol) without adequate alpha-blockade — this can precipitate a hypertensive emergency.
- **Do NOT** assume a small adrenal adenoma is benign — always rule out phaeochromocytoma with biochemistry before intervention.
- **Do NOT** use metoclopramide for nausea in a patient with undiagnosed phaeochromocytoma — it can trigger a severe crisis.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if**: Severe or "worst-ever" headache, palpitations with chest pain, breathlessness, visual disturbance, seizure, or faint.
- **Return if**: New or worsening hypertension in context of known adrenal mass waiting for surgery.
- **Follow-up**: Endocrinology for pre-op optimisation; surgical team for date of adrenalectomy. Once stable on alpha-blocker, proceed with surgery within 2–4 weeks.

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior (Registrar / SpR) if**:
  - BP >180/110 despite alpha-blocker, especially with headache or visual symptoms.
  - Heart rate >120 bpm with palpitations or chest pain.
  - Any arrhythmia (ECG change) or troponin rise.
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L.
- **Call ICU / Crash team if**:
  - Hypertensive crisis with end-organ failure (e.g., stroke, MI, pulmonary oedema, oliguria).
  - Seizure or GCS <13.
  - Sustained VT or VF.
- **Threshold for ICU admission**: Evidence of catecholamine-induced cardiomyopathy, multi-organ dysfunction, need for continuous IV alpha-blocker or vasopressors post-op.
